s2.q: A multiple pregnancy is defined as:
options: A pregnancy with more than 2 fetuses inside the uterus
A pregnancy with more than 1 fetus inside the uterus
A twin pregnancy that requires assisted reproductive technology
Any pregnancy where the fundal height exceeds gestational age by 4 cm
s2.explain: Multiple pregnancy is simply defined as a pregnancy with more than 1 fetus in the uterus. This includes twins (most common), triplets, and higher-order multiples. The management complexity and risks increase with each additional fetus.

options: Two separate eggs fertilized by two separate sperm
Division of a single fertilized zygote
Superfetation with two different ovulation cycles
In vitro fertilization with two embryos transferred
s2.explain: Monozygotic (identical) twins arise from the division of a single zygote and share the same genetic material. Their chorionicity and amnionicity depend on the timing of cleavage after fertilization. Monozygotic twins may be DCDA, MCDA, MCMA, or conjoined depending on cleavage timing.

options: The genetic makeup of a twin pregnancy
The number of chorions (equal to the number of placentas)
The number of amniotic sacs surrounding the fetuses
The timing of implantation after fertilization
s2.explain: Chorionicity is the number of chorions, which equals the number of placentas. Dichorionic twins have two separate placentas; monochorionic twins share one placenta. This distinction is critical because monochorionic twins are at risk for TTTS.

s2.q: Dizygotic twins share approximately what percentage of their genetic material?
s2.explain: Dizygotic (fraternal) twins result from two separate eggs fertilized by two separate sperm. Like any siblings, they share approximately 50% of their genetic material. They are never genetically identical, unlike monozygotic twins.
